title: Weeks of work ahead
caption: Daniel Wright stands in the back of his flood-damaged home in Swannanoa, N.C., on Oct. 26, 2024. After floodwaters filled his house with mud and debris, Wright spent weeks clearing out ruined belongings.

title: Left behind
caption: A mud-covered car sits abandoned in the Beacon Village neighborhood in Swannanoa, N.C., on Oct. 25, 2024. Cleanup was slow — Wright said it took days with 10 to 12 people shoveling heavy, thick mud out of flooded homes.

title: Down to the bones
caption: Daniel Wright stands in the exposed structure of his home in Swannanoa, N.C., on Oct. 25, 2024. Cleanup required heavy equipment, including a backhoe to clear debris and mud from around the house.

title: What remains
caption: A muddy football jersey hangs on a pile of debris in Beacon Village, Swannanoa, N.C., on Oct. 26, 2024. Wright recalled: “It was a long day — eight or nine hours felt like days. We were tired, wet, cold, and hungry, just trying to make sense of what was happening.”

title: Searching for what's lost
caption: Pat Gowan searches through debris at her family's Triple C Campground in Marion, N.C., on Oct. 8, 2024, hoping to recover belongings lost during Hurricane Helene. She and her husband had planned to celebrate the campground's 50th anniversary next year.

title: Scarred walls
caption: The exterior of a damaged building at Triple C Campground bears visible flood scars, a door half-buried in mud. Floodwaters in nearby Buck Creek surged to a record 21.44 feet — about 1,389% above normal.

title: Interior damage
caption: The interior of a house at Triple C Campground in Marion, N.C., on Oct. 8, 2024. Insulation and debris are scattered across dried mud, and parts of the ceiling have collapsed.

title: Clearing the way
caption: Volunteers from Samaritan’s Purse clear debris from a house in Swannanoa, N.C., on Oct. 26, 2024. The faith-based disaster relief organization mobilized volunteers to remove mud, salvage belongings, and prepare homes for repairs.

title: Submerged lives
caption: The flooded basement of a Swannanoa home on Oct. 26, 2024, with belongings submerged in mud. Program manager Jodie Yoder noted: “There’s always a huge rush of volunteers at the beginning of a disaster, and then it wanes when the media stops covering it.”

title: Sisters in the storm
caption: Reeda Ward (left) and her sister Nora Ward stand in a room packed with belongings moved from other parts of Reeda’s flood-damaged home in Saluda, N.C., on Oct. 27, 2024.

title: Hot meals, warm hearts
caption: Cayte Gowan serves free meals to residents and volunteers in the Ingles parking lot in Swannanoa, N.C., on Oct. 26, 2024. A professional chef who lost her job after Helene, Gowan said: “I like seeing the joy in people’s faces when they get hot food that’s well flavored.”

title: A warning on the pole
caption: Two handwritten signs on a utility pole in Swannanoa, N.C., read “Climate Change is Chaos” and “Climate Change is Death,” on Oct. 26, 2024.

title: Eroded ground
caption: An overturned vehicle rests on an eroded riverbank in Swannanoa, N.C., on Oct. 27, 2024. The storm triggered landslides, stripped away trees and topsoil, and left bedrock exposed.

title: Hope amid the wreckage
caption: A sign of encouragement stands amid the ruins of a destroyed gas station in Swannanoa, N.C., on Oct. 26, 2024. Smaller communities continue to face long roads to recovery.
